text: We present the case of a sixty nine year old man treated with TATME after stenting of an obstructed rectal cancer. In January, two thousand seventeen, this gentleman presented as an emergency to the hospital whilst on holiday in Sydney, Australia, and was found to have an obstructed cancer of the rectal sigmoid. An endoluminal stent was positioned to resolve the obstruction.

Once back in the UK, he was investigated with a PET and an MRI, which demonstrated a T-IV-N-II rectal cancer, approximately nine centimetres from the anal verge, with two potentially resectable liver metastases. After four cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy and restaging scans, he underwent removal of his tempted primary rectal cancer.

The surgeon begins the operation by mobilizing the splenic flexure, followed by dividing the inferior mesenteric vein and artery. The total mesorectal excision (TME) plane is entered using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection, encountering challenges due to inflammation from the stent. Anterolateral dissection is performed to include the peritoneal reflection, which appears involved by disease, and the anterior dissection progresses to the level of the seminal vesicles.

The surgeon positions a Shohan clamp on the sigmoid colon, initiating the perineal phase. The stent is clearly visible, and a purse-string suture is placed distal to it. The area is thoroughly washed with iodine solution before and after placing the suture. Circumferential marks are made with a diatomite hook, and dissection begins.
The surgeon performs circumferential dissection around the rectum, encountering difficulty in identifying surgical planes due to inflammation caused by the stent.
The surgeon completes the circumferential dissection around the rectum, leveling the lateral plane and finishing the dissection both posteriorly and anteriorly.
The surgeon creates a window on the right side of the rectum, breaking through the peritoneal cavity to connect with the abdominal dissection.
The surgeon extracts the bulky rectal specimen, affected by the presence of a stent, through an extended umbilical incision using a wound protector.

The surgeon performs an end-to-end anastomosis using a III-III EEA stapler. A defunctional loop ileostomy is created in the right iliac fossa. The total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection is confirmed to be within the mesorectal plane.
The histology confirmed the tumour staging T-IV-N-II with an extramural vascular invasion. The local excision was complete with a clear circumferential resection margin.

The patient didn't have any major complications and underwent liver metastasectomy in February, he's now waiting for the loop ileostomy to be closed. In conclusion, we think that the transrenal approach should be gold standard for resecting a stented rectal cancer to avoid the use of a linear stapler to transect the lower rectum, allowing a safe and precise DRM and avoiding the risk of cutting through the stent or fracturing the tumour.
